description: To 'phurewa' is to move dust, crumbs, leaves, or other loose material across a surface with repeated strokes of a broom or brush. The strokes may gather the material or push it out of the worked area. 'hewe' (wipe) clears through close contact with a hand or cloth; sweeping works through the returning reach of bristles.
articulatory_notes: Narrow the lips for unvoiced bilabial 'ph', pass breath between them, and round into high-back 'u'. Tap or briefly trill the tongue tip before stressed true-mid front 'e'. Round the lips and raise the back of the tongue for voiced 'w', then open into final central 'a'.
sound_symbolism: Deep bilabial breath in 'phu' turns through stressed 're'. Final 'wa' rounds at the lips and opens into the broadest vowel. The mouth changes direction at the centre and releases widely, a felt echo of the returning stroke.
usage_notes: A clause usually makes the worked surface the object. Loose material can take that place when the surface is already understood, and a broom or brush may appear with instrumental 'roe'. Used as a noun, the form names sweeping.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: A floor records the work plainly: one stroke changes one strip, and attention returns for the next.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Sweeping gathered ash for use, crumbs for animals, and dirt for removal before any of it could be called simply gone.

semantic_domains.activity: moving loose material with repeated brush strokes
